Scenario.overview: Communications Complex A sophisticated cyber attack targeting the communications sector has been orchestrated by an expert-level threat actor group. The attackers meticulously planned their approach, employing a multi-stage strategy involving extensive reconnaissance, resource development, and a wide array of techniques to achieve their objectives, including disrupting critical services and exfiltrating sensitive data.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Command & Control Server, User Account, Network Traffic, Public IP/Network Block, Domain Name Registrar, DNS Record, Process
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Web Shell, User Account Authentication, Network Traffic Flow, Network Service Scanning, Remote Desktop Protocol, Logon Session Creation, Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol, Process Creation

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engaged in active scanning activities to map the network topology, identifying potential entry points and vulnerabilities in the communications network.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers acquired and set up command and control servers outside the victim's jurisdiction to facilitate communication and control over compromised systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 439 Name: Manipulation During Distribution
stage.description: The attackers compromised a third-party vendor to introduce malware into the target's network via manipulated updates or patches.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Utilizing known vulnerabilities, the attackers exploited public-facing applications to execute malicious payloads and establish an initial foothold.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: To maintain persistence, the attackers deployed a web shell on compromised web servers, enabling remote access and control.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 645 Name: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: Using stolen credentials, the attackers escalated privileges within the network to gain higher levels of access and control.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: The attackers leveraged legitimate tools and system utilities already present in the environment to evade detection and analysis.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers extracted plaintext passwords and hashes from memory to gain unauthorized access to user accounts.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Mapping the internal network allowed the attackers to identify valuable targets for lateral movement and data collection.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 220 Name: Client-Server Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Using stolen credentials, the attackers moved laterally across the network via Remote Desktop Protocol to expand their reach.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 277 Name: Data Interchange Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers collected sensitive data from collaboration and storage repositories, compromising intellectual property and confidential communications.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 590 Name: IP Address Blocking
stage.description: Data was transferred to attacker-controlled servers using non-standard protocols, avoiding traditional monitoring mechanisms.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The attackers destroyed recovery environments and corrupted data integrity to maximize operational disruption.

Scenario.summary: The complex cyber attack on the communications sector involved a thorough reconnaissance phase followed by strategic resource development. Initial access was gained through supply chain compromise, leading to execution of malicious payloads. Persistence was maintained through web shells, privilege escalation was achieved using stolen credentials, defense evasion was performed using living-off-the-land techniques, and credentials were accessed through memory dumping. Lateral movement was facilitated through RDP, while sensitive data was collected and exfiltrated using alternative protocols. Finally, the impact stage saw the destruction of recovery environments and data integrity, causing significant disruption.
